item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's been so long since an update this comment is probably a bit necro and pointless but…

I really want to like this but there's a few glaring problems in my opinion.

Mainly the infectious aspect seems to be the most interesting and unique thing here but there's not a single mention of anything that caused an expansion. Could be an interesting bit about how they figured it out. Maybe losing an agent or researcher in the process.

No vote because I love the idea too much to downvote.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the image comes from Alamy and requires purchase for official use. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love the infectious time loop idea, but I'm so disappointed that there aren't any examples of the expanding field. The way it is now makes it sound like they're just assuming that the time loop expands, since there aren't examples of people or animals entering the time loop and expanding its area of influence.

I can imagine there should be at least one, maybe two expansion events: initial contact becomes trapped upon discovery, and perhaps again via accidental exposure from a negligent guard? Maybe even step up the containment procedures due to the accident, and upgrade from euclid to keter due to its potential effects?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Isn't the photo from that Coontz book "The Way We Never Were?"

Wait NO its from "Family Life In The 20th Century!"

item.title: Great post!
item.content:encoded: I love this scp! Great post! Its so cool, and mysterious. It raises a lot of questions too, which is neat. Like, what would happen if you flew a drone into the house; what experiment caused the explosion, ect. So cool. Great post! Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed.

I'd have something like "The family seems to follow the stereotypical dynamic consisting of the mother, father, and their two children, an older brother and younger sister." I do like the gag, though.

Upvoted ;)

item.title: Next step...
item.content:encoded:
As I mentioned in an earlier post, I love this SCP, but have you thought about including an addendum? Maybe an 05-level restricted entry that might delve deeper into Mr. Thompson's background to explain the "thermally active" object or perhaps some way to "release" the family?

I know, I know, the best SCPs are short, sweet, and enigmatic, but this one just makes such a darn good start to what could be a much deeper story. :)

item.title: Re: The Nuclear Family
item.content:encoded: Great job man. Enigmatic and brief. Feel free to add done addendum a as you think of them, like if Mr. Thompson had a background in physics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd take out the italicized note explaining that this is a typical 1950s American nuclear family, and it could use a good proofreading, but I think this is a pretty good time loop, all considered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
However, firing a bullet in from long range would likely be a disaster.

A high-powered rifle bullet would be a perfect surprise for all involved. A rocket/nuclear attack would be devastating to the secrecy of the whole operation, and possibly the Foundation, regardless of the SCP's AOE increase.

I mean, a nuke exploding every 8 seconds?

Granted, it requires prior knowledge of the anomaly, but for someone highly motivated that shouldn't be a problem.

Why the warehouse isn't internally heavily armored against this sort of thing is beyond me. Including a wider perimeter around it, with permanent guard patrols and an exclusion zone also seems like one of the things that could at the very least be done here.

I don't see why it can't be reclassified as a nuclear disaster zone. Build a small processing plant around the whole thing (mock it up), and then blow it to hell and claim chemical/radiological contamination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dont think enough focus was put on the infectious portion. its mentioned that it happens with no further information.

Also how much of a coincidence is it that this is right next to the nuclear family scp

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this entry but I have several issues with containment and it feels like the Foundation needs to be more proactive with this….

It seems that this operates in some sort of "Sphere of Influence" That being stated. How is what's happening underground being monitored? If a mole or an earthworm or whatever digs it's way in won't that cause an expansion as well?

Also what needs to be present for the effect to expand? Air Currents carry tons of particulates and even living matter. It seems like the first time a bird or bug flew over this before it was discovered it would have begun to expand rapidly.

Having read through the comments here I can see this was once listed as Keter, I actually probably agree with that classification more than Euclid.

By all appearances the Foundation is not currently containing the effects of this SCP, not to say that containment isn't possible the issue is much more research would need to be done before containment could be accomplished. This is a catch-22 as research in this case is next to impossible because of the consequences of interaction.

When constructing a big barn around an SCP and praying that a stray bug or bullet doesn't going flying through it's AOE thus wiping out all of existance is your only method of containment. I'd hardly call that containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is incredible. Upvote. Great job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why have you got a grudge against a decade?

Granted, I myself hate the '90's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it has to be assumed, for the sake of sanity, that objects on the mass/energy of a grain of dust, or a visible spectrum photon, don't trigger the effect.

However, firing a bullet in from long range would likely be a disaster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's Euclid because the time loop traps everyone who enters the area inside the loop, and expands with additional 'victims', whcih makes it dangerous because in the case of someone becoming trapped the whole containment procedure could potentially fail in short order and expose a lot of people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How far can a bullet travel in 4 seconds?
Hell, this thing should spread very far, given the speed of the light 4 seconds before it travels into the perimeter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There should be some notation of things that have gotten stuck as part of the time loop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it okay, but it's not different enough from 176 for me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey.

I did wonder what this would be classified as. It can be safe as it is relatively easy to contain and due to it's location is not likely to increase, though in theory it could be keter as it could grow larger should more and more add to it. Unlikely but possible. I have changed it to euclid for now, if anyone comments Ill be glad to hear why.

Reading through again appears did appear a lot, and for things that were either one thing or another (such as the mail box) So I have lowered the 'vagueness' of some of the obvious features of the house.

As for the four seconds, it is very arbitrary, it just seemed like a easy number to visualise.
I walked the distance of what I visualised the kitchen to be in four seconds. Junior is essentially saying 'going outside mom'. So it isnt so much as a conversing as briefly stating something, as you said.

hope the new changes sit better. Im gonna be working on touch ups through the week.

thanks for the advice. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made fixes. Learn how to make a possessive of a plural.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually like the "infectious time loop" idea more than the "completely impenetrable time loop", I just have a couple of nit-picks about it:

1) Having been yelled at about it myself, stick to a single classification (safe, euclid or keter). In this case I'd say Euclid, because while they have it more-or-less under control, something as innocuous as a wild critter escaping notice and zooming through the effect could suddenly double or triple its size.

2) "Appears" shows up way too often for a scientific report. Maybe putting a note at the start, "Individuals are believed to be as follows:" and then just listing off. Otherwise, things just "are", for instance you could say "the mailbox is listed as 143" instead of it "appearing" as such.

3) The four seconds isn't bad in-and-of itself, it just seems to be a…. Well, very busy four seconds, especially in the second-by-second breakdown. The simple thing would be to expand the time of the loop, but sticking with 4/8, maybe have an overall assessment without clear delineation. The one bit that stands out to me is Junior "conversing" with mom; the most he could say in one second would be something like "Be back later!" and that's if he's rushing it. Maybe "states something" instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mythbuster style
test, replicate results, blow it the hell up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hey thanks for the advice.

I'm currently doing tests for uni this week so I wont be able to update too severely but I plan to properly edit this with the suggestions I have received from the comments.
I understand there is a lot of similarity to 176, simply because they are time loops, amongst other things. When I can, I will rewrite this to make the differentiation between them clearer, which will hopefully means they will be considered separate concepts.
I shall go announce this now, should have done that first. Sorry.

Thanks for the advice, it is greatly appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would upvote it if not for the similarity to 176. I do like the number you chose, it invokes my hatred of the 50's and makes me more invested in the idea of blowing up a piece of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree, I like the idea of someone being trapped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First: announce this in the announcement thread. Some people use it as main notification channel for new articles. And when those people finally find your article through random wandering, their first desire is to downvote it because it wasn't announced in the announcement thread.

Second: concept was done before. I wouldn't say that it was done better, because I personally like this version more. But it was done before, and that's pretty big problem in my opinion. Expansion is a nice touch, not because it makes it more dangerous or hard to contain, but because it solves all the fridge logic present in 176 regarding the interactions with the boundary of the phenomenon. But it isn't enough. Try to think of something else to differentiate this from 176. Time loops can be used in variety of interesting ways. This, and 176, are mostly generic timeloop with nothing interesting besides the fact that it is timeloop.

Third: Writing is mostly alright, at least by the time I got there. There are some places in which phrasing is awkward and I had to reread some sentences to understand what you mean, but it's not the bubble gum. 1950-1-4 is better than calling them by names, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very similar to… etc.

"Spectral analysis of the explosion in inconclusive as to the cause of the explosion."

I understand the Euclid, as it's can still be expanding. (Like, from air that interacts.)

Lose the excess tags.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted for the precise same reason. Why is this even euclid/keter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hop on into the Realtime chat for help! It's what we're here for!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems people like the concept but aren't too impressed with the writing, which is something i have taken into consideration and will do a complete rewrite asap, I just wanted to see if people liked the idea, and for my first SCP, I don't think I've done too badly.

Dude this should be like four separate sentences :|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thats fair enough. I do like the human aspect to it, other wise its just a bunch of things going in circles which is virtually anomalous events worthy. I do think I need to reword most of it though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. It's actually better for me, if you keep the names in. 1950-1-4 would take away something I can't quite articulate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because the explosion is nuclear, and it's a nuclear family- no, that can't be it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems people like the concept but aren't too impressed with the writing, which is something i have taken into consideration and will do a complete rewrite asap, I just wanted to see if people liked the idea, and for my first SCP, I don't think I've done too badly.

And the choice of number, not particularly. I liked it. Thats about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but It should be put to euclid, or even safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know why it's keter.

But I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I might argue that the concept is different enough to stand on it's own, but certainly not in its present form. Beyond that, I'm with Yoric on this one.

EDIT: And was the choice of item number meant to be humorous? >.>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Decent, but not really different enough from the other time loop we have to earn an upvote. The writing is awful, though, and needs heavy proofreading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i would remove the reference to "nuclear" within the article itself….that particular gag is better if it's implied, realized by the reader rather than fed to them directly(plus i dont see how it would be scientifically relevant anyway).

i think it would be worth mentioning any and all people who are trapped in the time loop besides the family. if there are none, then why is it mentioned?

i like this a lot. i dont mind having more than one time loop of sorts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the votes :D

Yeah the family are a bit of a by product but as i developed the idea, more and more backstory kinda came to me, they were a good physically way of descripting the effect, in my mind at least. plus i think writing out more information on them could be interesting to read and write.

any more comments would be appreciated.

also i know there are similarities to 176, but i feel it differs in the fact this is essentially an infectious time loop. you interract with it and you become part of it, which i feel is worthy enough to warrant it as a seperate scp concept…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not too big on this. Not really sure why the family is even involved. They seem to just be random victims of the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like this one in spite of the similarity to SCP-176. This is neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: not bad. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found it entertaining. You have a number of missed articles. Look through and find cases where you can add an "a" or "an" before nouns.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made some changes, pretty big ones glaring me in the face. Got swept up in trying to write american english, god knows why. Hopefully with these changes it a bit more of a better read, though i feel there are still something i could tweak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
White = Caucasian
All family members should probably be labeled "SCP-1950-X" (1-4), not referred to by their names.
Use metric!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvote , as I think we have a much better time loop - one of my favourites,SCP-176

item.title: The Nuclear Family
item.content:encoded: So this is a nuclear family from the 1950s stuck in a 4s time loop. The difference is that anything that enters a certain distance of the time loop becomes part of the loop and can extend the distance of loop. Any advice or criticisms would be appreciated, this is my first scp.
